title: Mk.gee: The Guitarist Who Liquified Sound
author: Leelou Blanc
description: There's a moment about a minute into "Are You Looking Up," the opening track of Mk.gee's debut album.
image_alt: Mk.gee: The Guitarist Who Liquified Sound
body:
[spotify:artist/7tr9pbgNEKtG0GQTKe08Tz]

## A Sound That Shouldn't Work But Does

There is a moment about a minute into "Are You Looking Up," the opening track of Mk.gee's debut album *Two Star & the Dream Police*, where the guitar loses its structural integrity. It does not break. It melts. The notes are still there, technically, but they slide against each other in ways that suggest a different relationship with time than conventional music operates within. The listener's first instinct is to question whether the playback is functioning correctly. Then the second instinct arrives: this is entirely deliberate, and it is extraordinary.

This is what makes Michael Gordon Leen, who records as Mk.gee, genuinely interesting. He found a guitar tone that no one had quite found before, and built an entire emotional world from it.

## The Sound Itself

Leen's guitar playing is processed heavily, pitch-shifted, time-stretched, treated with effects that blur the line between string instrument and synthesizer. But unlike a lot of heavily-processed guitar work, there is never a sense that the processing is hiding technical limitation or compensating for something. The processing is the statement. The distortion and smearing of pitch are baked into the compositional thinking from the start.

The technical specifics matter here. The pitch-shifting Leen uses is not the clean transposition of a digital pedal but something wetter and more unstable, closer to the artifacts you get when tape runs inconsistently or when pitch correction software is pushed past its design parameters. The result is notes that have halos, that exist in a slight cloud of themselves. When two of these notes sound together, the interaction between their halos creates a beating, a pulse within the sustained sound that gives even static chords a feeling of internal movement.

The result sits in a strange middle space between ambient music, R&B, and alternative. It draws comparisons to Arca, to early Frank Ocean production, to a certain strain of late-night emotional electronics. But it sounds like none of those things exactly. It sounds like Mk.gee.

His background is relevant here: years spent as a session musician and behind-the-scenes collaborator, absorbing the vocabulary of multiple genres without being claimed by any of them. That accumulated fluency is what makes *Two Star & the Dream Police* sound so technically confident while remaining emotionally raw. He knows exactly what the rules are. The departures from them are informed.

## Two Star & the Dream Police

Released in February 2024, the album arrived with the kind of critical reception that makes you wonder where this person had been hiding. The record is quiet in the way that very dense things can be quiet. Every element is restrained, nothing is wasted, and the cumulative effect of that restraint is considerable pressure. You feel the weight of what is not being said.

The lyrics match the music in emotional register: personal, oblique, dealing in the textures of loss and desire without ever quite stating what is actually being lost or wanted. Lines are incomplete. Pronouns shift. The listener is never given a stable position from which to understand the narrative because there is no stable position. The instability is the content.

The sequencing matters. The album moves through emotional states the way a long drive through an unfamiliar city does, disorienting at first, then gradually legible, then unexpectedly moving in ways you did not anticipate.

## Before the Album: The Collaboration Years

Before the album, Leen had been circulating through high-profile collaborations quietly enough that most listeners did not know his name. Production and guitar credits across the world of experimental R&B and pop, work that shaped the sound of other people's music without announcing itself. Session work requires a specific kind of suppression of self, a willingness to make your instincts invisible in service of someone else's vision. Doing that well for years builds technical fluency. It also builds pressure. The identity that had been distributed across other people's records finally had a single place to consolidate. *Two Star & the Dream Police* is what that consolidation sounds like.

## Emotional Intelligence as Aesthetic

What separates Mk.gee from a lot of artists working in adjacent territory is that the experimentalism never functions as experimentation for its own sake. The guitar processing is strange, yes, but it is strange in service of something. There is an emotional precision to the album that goes beyond technical novelty. Every unusual choice lands in the right emotional register, which means the choices are not arbitrary. They are calibrated.

The comparison to Frank Ocean is instructive even if the sounds are different. Both are artists for whom the production is the emotional content, where how something is made and what it is saying are inseparable. *Two Star & the Dream Police* operates in that mode: the texture of the music is the argument the music is making. To strip away the processing and play the songs clean would not reveal their essence. It would destroy it. The production is the song.

## A Different Kind of Guitar Hero

The cult of guitar virtuosity has been one of the odder persistent features of rock discourse, the idea that technical facility is the thing that matters most, that speed and precision are the relevant measures of achievement. Leen plays against that tradition almost entirely. The facility is present but hidden beneath processing and restraint. What is foregrounded instead is sensitivity, to atmosphere, to pacing, to the emotional weight of a single held note allowed to sustain until its overtones become audible.

That shift in emphasis, from what you can do to what you feel, is exactly what makes *Two Star & the Dream Police* feel like a record that matters. Not just as a debut. As a statement about what guitar music might still become. The instrument spent decades being associated with aggression, volume, and demonstrative technique. Leen uses it to communicate fragility and interiority. The gap between those registers is where the album lives.
